nativeRange.habitat: Sandy bottoms on shallow tropical reefs, typically 3–15 m depth; often observed solitarily or in pairs

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on small live invertebrates picked from sand and rock — copepods, amphipods, and other tiny crustaceans. Sources widely advise an established reef system with a thriving pod population, ideally supported by an attached refugium. Some specimens have been reported to gradually accept frozen mysis, enriched brine shrimp, glassworms, or bloodworms with patient training, but refusal of prepared foods is common on arrival. Several small feedings per day are generally advised in systems without sufficient natural prey.

avoidWith: other dragonets (except mated pairs introduced simultaneously)
competitive feeders (dottybacks, anthias, basslets, hungry wrasses)

summary: A small, sand-dwelling dragonet from the Western Central Pacific marked by black-and-orange dorsal fins and orange streaks on a pale body; generally considered suitable for advanced reef keepers who can sustain a live copepod and amphipod population.
careNotes:
The Orange and Black Dragonet (Dactylopus kuiteri) is a close relative of the Mandarin and Fingered Dragonets and shares the family's defining husbandry challenge: long-term success depends on a steady supply of small live invertebrates. Aquarists generally advise a well-established reef aquarium with abundant live rock for grazing, a deep live sand bed (the fish uses finger-like pelvic fin rays to walk along the substrate, and has been reported to bury itself with only its eyes exposed), and ideally an attached refugium to keep pod populations replenished.

This species tends to be peaceful with dissimilar tankmates but cannot compete with fast or aggressive feeders. Sources advise against keeping it with dottybacks, anthias, larger wrasses, and similar competitive species. Multiple specimens have been reported to cohabitate if introduced simultaneously, with a single male per display tank being the usual recommendation; males have been observed to be aggressive toward each other.

Reef safety: generally considered reef safe — has not been widely reported to harm corals or sessile invertebrates, though it may consume small motile invertebrates as part of its natural diet.

Copper sensitivity: as a scaleless, mucus-covered dragonet, this species is generally considered highly intolerant of copper-based medications, consistent with other Callionymidae.

Cross-source notes — temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F) for aquarium keeping, while FishBase wild-habitat data gives 28.3–29.2°C. The aquarium-keeper-focused range of 23–27°C is used here as a reasonable middle ground.

Cross-source notes — tank size: LiveAquaria's stated minimum is around 50 US gal (~189 L). Given the species' slow feeding behaviour and reliance on a productive pod population, the tank recommendation here is biased upward to 250 L to provide adequate live rock, refugium space, and prey-replenishment capacity for long-term keeping. No other tested secondary (BlueZoo, Reef2Reef wiki) returned a species-specific page during research; sizing is therefore based on the single retail source plus the playbook's cautious-larger rule for slow-feeding dragonets.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Dactylopus kuiteri has not been widely documented. As with other dragonets, paired specimens may occasionally engage in evening spawning ascents toward the water surface, but rearing larvae through to juvenile stage is generally considered to be exceptionally difficult and has rarely if ever been reported outside specialist research contexts.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page confirmed: scientific name (Dactylopus kuiteri), care level (difficult → mapped to advanced), temperament (peaceful), reef compatible (yes), adult size (6 in / ~15 cm), minimum tank (50 gal / ~189 L), diet (carnivore — brine shrimp, bloodworms, glassworms, small invertebrates), temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, specific gravity 1.020–1.025, and behavioural notes (uses pelvic fins to walk, may bury in sand, multiple specimens can cohabitate if introduced simultaneously). The collections index page https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/dragonets was checked first but lacked species-specific numeric data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verified: scientific name, family (Callionymidae), order (Callionymiformes), maximum length (15.0 cm TL), depth range (3–15 m), native distribution (Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines — Western Central Pacific), temperature range (28.3–29.2°C wild), tropical climate, demersal habitat (sandy bottoms; solitary or in pairs), carnivore (trophic level 3.3), sexual dimorphism (males with elongated first dorsal spine), IUCN status Least Concern. FishBase did not supply pH, salinity, dKH, or aquarium tank size. Wild temperature differs from LiveAquaria's aquarium recommendation (28.3–29.2°C wild vs 22.2–25.6°C captive); a middle-ground 23–27°C range is used.

fish.breedingNotes: Larval rearing of Callionymidae is widely considered exceptionally difficult; no consistent reports of successful captive rearing of D. kuiteri to juvenile stage in the hobby literature.
